point_001: The technical novelty of the proposed method is limited; the core idea of using language/text representations to approximate human similarity judgments has been explored in prior work (e.g., Marjieh et al. 2022), and this paper is largely an extension with more experiments rather than a fundamentally new contribution.
point_002: The paper lacks comparison against supervised baselines, such as training a model on a small number of pairwise human judgments to map domain-specific representations to human similarity scores, which would contextualize the unsupervised text-based approach.
point_003: The distinction between using tags versus captions is insufficiently analyzed; it is unclear whether performance gains come from the richer content of full captions or from the more expressive language models used to encode them. A simple linearization of tags (e.g., 'A photo of tag1, tag2, tag3') fed to an LLM would clarify this.
point_004: The paper does not explore a fully LLM-based similarity setup where both text descriptions are jointly fed to a large language model (e.g., GPT-3) to directly output a similarity rating, which is a natural and relevant baseline.
point_005: The study does not investigate low-supervision regimes where fewer than N captions are collected and a model is trained on those sparse annotations to map from a modality to text, which would be practically valuable.
point_006: The paper does not justify the exclusive use of English, despite the existence of many other languages. Given linguistic diversity and differences in grammar, polysemy, and dataset availability, the choice of English warrants explicit motivation.
point_007: All behavioral experiments used US-based participants (N=1,492), raising concerns about cultural generalizability; the paper does not discuss whether findings would hold across participants from different cultural or linguistic backgrounds.
point_008: Key terminology such as 'representational similarity' is not defined in the introduction, making the paper harder to follow for readers unfamiliar with the field.
point_009: The connection between the paper's title and its abstract/content is not immediately clear, and the title could be revised to better reflect the paper's actual contributions and scope.
point_010: The paper ends with a 'Discussion' section but lacks a dedicated 'Conclusion' section, which would help summarize the key findings and contributions more explicitly.
point_011: The paper does not report the time participants take to complete the STEP-Tag annotation task, which would strengthen claims about the efficiency and cost-effectiveness of the proposed pipeline relative to caption writing.
point_012: The paper does not evaluate whether stacked (language + DNN) representations improve performance on standard downstream tasks such as image classification, which would further validate the practical utility of the approach.
